what Is the best wheat grown in


the spring wheat section?" I asked


Professor w. M. Mays, assistant sec


the best authority on wheat breeding


in the world.


minnesota No. 1863. Here is what
the wheat experts of the st. Louis
exposition thought of it," and he
handed me letter he had just re-
ceived, being an official notice that
this wheat had won a grand prize,


fnd that he, as originator of it, would
receive grand prize medal.
This wheat is better than others
he continued, because it has been
bred for two purposes. To make a
high quality of -four, and big yields
of grain. We test new variety for


both qualities before sending it out
This wheat was bred at the Min


nesota experiment station from care
fully selected stock. During the
eighth and ninth years we made ex-
tensive field tests of it, and in 1902


sent GU the first seed, 800 lots of four
bushels each.


This seed was sold at about dou-
BILE the market value of wheat, and
| only to those who agreed to grow as
much grain as possible for distribu-
ton as seed.


"The results have been very flat
tering. Yields have been three to
five bushels more per acre from No
100 than from the other varieties
grown alongside of it. This spring
at least 100000 acres of it will be
sown. At a conservative estimate
this should give the growers 1000.
600 bushels of increase over what
| they would get if other varieties were
sown. Next year there will be 1,500,-
| 600 acres of wheat No. 169 grown in
| the northwest, and by the following
year it will be grown generally all
over the spring wheat section, adding
several million dollars to the gross
income from this product.


This is what plant breeding has
done. We were 10 years producing
this variety. 1t took a vast amount
of labor. Hundreds Of thousands of
| wheat plants We're examined, and
records made on many of them. But
look at the result The increased
value of the wheat crop this year


will more than pay for all the work
we have done in plant breeding. and


we have not confined our work to


wheat alone.


I'VE hope to take this variety and


from it develop something enough


better to displace it. In fact we are


working on that now. but it will be
